purposes.description: Decompose time series into trend and cyclical components using the Hodrick-Prescott filter with appropriate lambda selection by observation frequency, including log-transformation for level variables.

primitives.evidence: HP filter implementation uses numpy (third-party) for matrix operations: np.zeros, np.eye, np.linalg.solve, matrix transposition and multiplication (D.T @ D). Also np.log for log-transformation.
Understanding the HP filter minimization problem, selecting lambda (100/1600/14400) by frequency, and interpreting cycle values as percentage deviations from trend.

purposes.description: Decompose time series with periodic patterns into trend, seasonal, and residual components using additive or multiplicative models.

primitives.evidence: Uses statsmodels.tsa.seasonal.seasonal_decompose (third-party) with model='additive'/'multiplicative' and period parameter. Also uses pandas Series.
Choosing between additive (constant seasonal amplitude) and multiplicative (amplitude grows with level) models based on data characteristics.

purposes.description: Detect anomalies in time series data using rolling z-score method and normalized anomaly index, classifying as spike, dip, or level shift.

primitives.evidence: Uses pandas (third-party) for rolling window statistics (s.rolling().mean(), s.rolling().std()), numpy for np.tanh in anomaly index computation.
Z-score computation: (value - rolling_mean) / rolling_std, threshold comparison, and anomaly_index = 100 * tanh(deviation / std_residual) normalization.

purposes.description: Compute cross-correlations between cyclical components of related time series at multiple lags to identify lead-lag relationships.

primitives.evidence: Uses numpy np.corrcoef for correlation at each lag, scipy.stats.pearsonr for significance testing. Array slicing for lag alignment.
Interpreting cross-correlations at different lags: positive lag means y leads x, understanding co-movement vs correlation distinction.
